summary: Kernel is memory-bound at 86.9% DRAM throughput with severe long scoreboard stalls from memory latency
reasoning: The kernel achieves 86.9% of peak DRAM bandwidth (2.13 TB/s out of 3.9 TB/s) while compute throughput is only 8.5%. The dominant stall reason is long scoreboard at 59.54%, indicating warps are waiting for global memory operations to complete. With 0% L1 cache hit rate and only 50.7% L2 hit rate, the kernel is streaming data directly from HBM with minimal cache reuse.

root_causes.cause: Poor data locality causing excessive DRAM traffic with no L1 cache reuse

evidence.interpretation: Zero L1 cache hits means every access goes to L2 or DRAM
Only half of L2 accesses hit, causing significant DRAM traffic
High DRAM bandwidth consumption at 2.13 TB/s

root_causes.cause: Memory latency stalls dominating execution due to insufficient latency hiding

evidence.interpretation: Nearly 60% of stalls are from waiting on global memory loads
Low occupancy at 30% limits ability to hide memory latency with other warps

recommended_fixes.fix: Increase occupancy by reducing register usage (currently 94 registers per thread limits to 5 blocks per SM) through register spilling or smaller tile sizes, and consider fusing the two-pass algorithm into a single pass to halve memory traffic
recommended_fixes.rationale: Higher occupancy provides more warps to hide memory latency, and reducing passes from 2 to 1 in the large-C kernel would cut DRAM traffic in half, directly improving the memory-bound bottleneck
